standard_description:
Decompose a fraction into a sum of fractions with the same denominator in more than one way, recording each decomposition by an equation. Justify decompositions, e.g., by using a visual fraction model. Examples: 3/8 = 1/8 + 1/8 + 1/8 ; 3/8 = 1/8 + 2/8 ; 2 1/8 = 1 + 1 + 1/8 = 8/8 + 8/8 + 1/8.
Grade 4 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 100.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Decompose the following fraction in three different ways. Record the decompositions with equations.
$${{7\over8}}$$

Problem 1
There are a few more ingredients in Lin’s family recipe for cookies, listed below. Write an addition equation and a multiplication equation to show how she can use the
$$\frac14$$
-cup measure to measure the amount of each ingredient below.
a.
$$\frac94$$
cups chocolate chips
b.
$$2\frac34$$
cup flour
Problem 2
Decompose each of the following fractions as a sum of unit fractions, a multiple of a unit fraction, and a sum of non-unit fractions.
a.
$${{8\over2}}$$
b.
$$3\frac{1}{4}$$
c.
$${{12\over5}}$$
Problem 3
Compose each of the following expressions.
a.
$${{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}}$$
b.
$${{3\over5}+{2\over5}+{6\over5}}$$
c.
$$12\times\frac{1}{4}$$
d.
$$9\times\frac{1}{2}$$

Problem 1
Mrs. Fowler knew that the perimeter of the soccer field was
$${{1\over6}}$$
mile. She walked
$$2{{{1\over6}}}$$
miles in total. How many times did she walk around the field? Show or explain your work.
Problem 2
Convert the following mixed numbers to fractions greater than 1. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${3{1\over2}}$$
b.
$${4{1\over4}}$$
c.
$${5{3\over5}}$$
Problem 3
Ben wrote the mixed number
$$7\frac{1}{3}$$
as a fraction. Here is his work:
###TABLE0###
Explain what Ben did in each step.

Problem 1
Ms. Giordano, a high school soccer coach, tells her players to run between
$$3$$
and
$$4$$
miles every week over the summer in preparation for the fall season. Kayla runs
$${{1\over2}}$$
mile every day. Will Kayla run enough each week for Ms. Giordano? How do you know?
Problem 2
Convert the following fractions to mixed numbers. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{8\over3}}$$
b.
$${{15\over4}}$$
c.
$${{32\over6}}$$
Problem 3
Alice wrote the fraction
$${{36\over5}}$$
as a mixed number. Here is her work:
###TABLE0###
Explain what Alice did in each step.

Write each mixed number as an equivalent fraction greater than 1. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${3{1\over4}}$$
b.
$${2{3\over5}}$$
c.
$${4{2\over9}}$$

Decompose the following fraction as a sum of unit fractions as well as a multiple of unit fractions. Record the decompositions with equations.
$$\frac69$$

Problem 1
Decompose the following fractions as a sum of unit fractions and a multiple of a unit fraction.
a.
$${{10\over2}}$$
b.
$${2{3\over4}}$$
Problem 2
Compose the following fractions.
a.
$${{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}+{1\over3}}$$
b.
$${18\times{1\over5}}$$

Problem 1
Lin’s family recipe for cookies also calls for
$$\frac64$$
cups chopped nuts. Write an addition equation and a multiplication equation to show how she can use the
$$\frac14$$
-cup measure to measure the amount of chopped nuts.
Problem 2
a.   On each number line, draw two “jumps” to show how to use thirds to make a sum of
$$\frac53$$
. Then, write an equation to represent each combination of jumps.
###IMAGE0###
###IMAGE1###
b.   Noah draws the following number line and writes:
$$\frac53 = \frac33 + \frac23$$
and
$$\frac53= 1+\frac23$$
. Which equation is correct? Explain your reasoning.
###IMAGE2###
c.   Write
$$\frac64$$
as a sum of a whole number and a fraction. Use a number line if it would help you.
Problem 3
a.   On each number line, draw “jumps” to show how to use eighths to make a sum of
$$1\frac38$$
. Then, write an equation to represent each combination of jumps.
###IMAGE3###
###IMAGE4###
b.   Explain why
$${1{3\over8}}={11\over8}$$
using a number line or words.

Task
Which of the following sums are equal to $\frac{22}{17}$?
$\frac{5}{17}+\frac{4}{17}+\frac{3}{17}+\frac{10}{17}$
$\frac{3}{17}+\frac{8}{17}+\frac{3}{17}+\frac{10}{17}$
$\frac{6}{17}+\frac{4}{17}+\frac{3}{17}+\frac{5}{17}+\frac{2}{17}+\frac{2}{17}$
$\frac{12}{17}+\frac{10}{17}$
$\frac{1}{17}+\frac{1}{17}+\frac{9}{17}+\frac{3}{17}$
Find another way to write $\frac{22}{17}$ as a sum of fractions.

Write each fraction as an equivalent mixed number.
a.
$${{11\over5}}$$
b.
$${{13\over4}}$$
c.
$${{16\over3}}$$

Decompose the following fractions (i) as a sum of unit fractions, (ii) as a multiple of a unit fraction, and (iii) in at least one other way.
a.
$${{7\over6}}$$
b.
$${1{3\over4}}$$

Problem 1
Lin is learning to make cookies using a family recipe. She has only one measuring cup that measures
$$\frac1 4$$
cup. Show how Lin could use the cup to measure the amount of each ingredient below.
a.
$$\frac 3 4$$
cups sugar
b.   1 cup butter
Problem 2
Decompose the fraction represented below into a sum of unit fractions and a multiple of that unit fraction. Record the decompositions with equations.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
Decompose the following fractions into a sum of unit fractions and a multiple of that unit fraction, recording each with an equation.
a.
$$\frac{4}{5}$$
b.
$$\frac{3}{3}$$

Problem 1
Lin later found a
$$\frac34$$
-cup measuring cup. Show how she could use the
$$\frac34$$
and
$$\frac14$$
measuring cups to measure the right amount of each ingredient below.
a.
$$\frac34$$
cups sugar
b.   1 cup butter
Problem 2
On each number line, draw two “jumps” to show how to use sixths to make a sum of
$$\frac66$$
. Then, write an equation to represent each combination of jumps.
###IMAGE0###
###IMAGE1###
Problem 3
a.   Use different combinations of tenths to make a sum of
$$\frac{9}{10}$$
.
$$\frac{9}{10}$$
= ___ + ___ + ___ + ___ + ___
$$\frac{9}{10}$$
= ___ + ___ + ___ + ___
$$\frac{9}{10}$$
= ___ + ___ + ___
$$\frac{9}{10}$$
= ___ + ___
b.   Write different ways to use fifths to make a total of
$$\frac 55$$
. How many can you think of? Write an equation for each combination.
c.   Is it possible to write any fraction with a denominator of 8 as a sum of other eighths? Explain or show your reasoning.

items.question_content:
**Which fractions add together to make $\dfrac{13}{2}$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: All of the fractions have the same denominator, $2$, so the parts have the same size. We are ready to add.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac{1}{2}}+\greenD{\dfrac{4}{2}} +\purpleD{\dfrac{8}{2}} =\dfrac{13}{2}$

items.question_content:
Lincoln wants to mix $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of a liter of red paint with $\dfrac{7}{8}$ of a liter of blue paint to make purple paint.

**How many liters of purple paint will Lincoln make by mixing the two colors?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] liters
hints:
To find the amount of $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$ paint, we can add the liters of $\redD{\text{red}}$ paint to the liters of $\blueD{\text{blue}}$ paint:

$\redD{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \blueD{\dfrac{7}{8}}$

Lincoln will make ${\dfrac{10}{8}}$ liters of $\purpleD{\text{purple}}$ paint.

This can also be written as ${\dfrac{5}{4}}$ liters or ${1\dfrac{1}{4}}$ liters.

items.question_content:
Travis just started high school, and he wants to manage his time wisely.  He schedules $\dfrac{3}{2}$ hours each day for homework, $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of an hour each day for chores at home, and $\dfrac{17}{2}$ hours each day for sleep.

** How many hours each day are scheduled for these activities?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] hours
hints:
To find out the total hours Travis has scheduled for these activities, we can add the fractions:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{2}} + \greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{17}{2}}$

Travis schedules ${\dfrac{21}{2}}$ hours each day for these activities.

This can also be written as ${10\dfrac{1}{2}}$ hours.

items.question_content:
Bjorn is baking his favorite dessert.  The recipe calls for $\dfrac{5}{8}$ of a stick of butter, but he wants to double the recipe.  

**How much butter will Bjorn need?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] sticks
hints:
To find out how many sticks of butter Bjorn needs, we can add the fraction $\dfrac{5}{8}$ twice:

$\blueD{\dfrac{5}{8}} + \greenD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

Bjorn will need  $\dfrac{{10}}{8}$ of a stick of butter.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{{5}}{4}$ sticks or ${1\dfrac{1}{4}}$ sticks.

items.question_content:
Tyler and Katie started a lemonade stand to raise money.  They donated $\dfrac{2}{10}$ of their profits to their school library, $\dfrac{1}{10}$ to the animal shelter, and $\dfrac{4}{10}$ to the food bank.  They saved the rest to buy materials for their next project.

**What fraction of their profits did Tyler and Katie donate?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find out what fraction of the profits were donated, we can add the fractions:

$\blueD{\dfrac{2}{10}} + \green{\dfrac{1}{10}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{10}}$
Tyler and Katie donated $\dfrac{{7}}{10}$ of their profits.

items.question_content:
One glass of milk gives you $\dfrac{3}{10}$ of the recommended amount of calcium per day.  

**$3$ glasses of milk will give you what fraction of the recommended amount of calcium per day?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find the fraction of recommended calcium from $3$ glasses of milk, we can add the fraction you will get from each glass of milk $3$ times:


$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{10}} + \green{\dfrac{3}{10}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{10}}$
If you drink $3$ glasses of milk a day, you will get $\dfrac{{9}}{10}$of the recommended amount of calcium.

 **How much do Joanna's books weigh altogether?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] kilograms
hints:
To find out how many kilograms the books weigh altogether, we can add their weights:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{8}} + \greenD{\dfrac{11}{8}}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{8}}$

Joanna's books weigh $\dfrac{{19}}{8}$ kilograms.

This can also be written as ${2\dfrac{3}{8}}$ kilograms.

items.question_content:
A child's ladder is made of $3$ sections.  Each section is $\dfrac{3}{4}$ meters long.

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
To find out the total length of the ladder, we can add the lengths of the $3$ sections:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{4}} + \greenD{\dfrac{3}{4}} + \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{4}}$

The length of the child's ladder is $\dfrac{{9}}{4}$ meters.

This can also be written as ${2\dfrac{1}{4}}$ meters.

hints:

items.question_content:
Sven is reading a book while on vacation.  So far, he has read $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of the book. He wants to finish $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of the book by the end of vacation.

**What fraction of the book does Sven want to read between now and the end of vacation?**

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:
To find the fraction of the book that Sven wants to read, we can subtract the fraction he has read from the total amount he wants to read:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{6}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$

Sven wants to read another $\dfrac{2}{6}$ of his book while he is on vacation.

We can also write this as $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of his book.

items.question_content:

[[☃ input-number 1]]
hints:

$\blueD{\dfrac{97}{100}} - \greenD{\dfrac{87}{100}}$

Kira finished $\dfrac{10}{100}$ of the problems in the second hour.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of the problems.

items.question_content:

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{5}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$
The length of the lizard without the tail is $\dfrac{2}{5}$ meters.

We can also write this as $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of his book.

Kira finished $\dfrac{10}{100}$ of the problems in the second hour.

This can also be written as $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of the problems.

items.question_content:

$\blueD{\dfrac{3}{5}} - \greenD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$
The length of the lizard without the tail is $\dfrac{2}{5}$ meters.
